text: Here we investigate for the first time how the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) affects the hydrolytic activity of a cellulose-degrading enzyme, the enzyme β-glucosidase (BG), using two pre-reduced Fe-bearing clay minerals and one pre-reduced iron oxide. Experiments were carried out at pH 5 and 7, under both anoxic and oxic conditions. In the absence of ROS (i.e., anoxic conditions), enzyme adsorption to mineral surfaces was associated with both lower initial activities and longer-lasting activity, possibly reflecting a trade-off between catalytic performance and enzyme stability. Under aerobic conditions, ROS such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were detected, with their amounts highly correlated with the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation in the reduced minerals. These ROS significantly impaired enzyme catalysis. This enzymatic activity loss was attributed to a combined effects of ROS-induced protein unfolding and enzyme structural degradation. Therefore, under aerobic conditions, the detrimental effects of ROS on enzyme catalysis related to Fe(II)-bearing minerals clearly dominated the protective role of the enzyme adsorption to minerals surfaces. Our work thereby identifies for the first time a new enzymatic activity-inactivating mechanism under aerobic redox environment with implications to the estimation of the bioavailable enzyme activity in the dynamic soil and sediment environments.
